**Visualizing the Update**

I'm currently focused on the image's key request: crafting a heart icon outline matching the app's visual style. The user's goal is clear: to maintain design consistency. I'm exploring potential shapes and line weights to ensure the icon seamlessly integrates with the app's overall aesthetic and the "Market Intelligence" screen context.

I see the existing icon is a generic heart; I need to refine it. I also noticed that the tabs are rendered as buttons with some styling. The goal is to provide a heart outline that matches this visual style.


**Refining the Heart Outline**

I've examined the `MarketView.tsx` code snippet, particularly the tab button styling. I'm focusing on the `IconComponent` and the conditional rendering based on `isWishlist` and `isActive` states. The existing heart icon needs an aesthetic uplift. I'm contemplating how to create a heart outline to replace the generic heart. The design must integrate seamlessly with the tab's visual styles, like the active/inactive state changes, as well as the text font and the visual style the user requested.

I'm focusing now on the visual style of the existing app UI elements in the `MarketView` header. The "Wishlist" heart icon needs a style overhaul, and the screenshot helps, but the "I <3" mobile view reference could just be the icon itself. Specifically, I see how it handles the "I <3" mobile view. It seems this could be more dynamic. The styling now inherits from the button's text color: `text-midnight` when active, `text-slate-text` when inactive. This achieves the desired monochrome look with app theme integration.

I've realized that leveraging the default `Lucide` icon behavior simplifies the code. The existing button styling already manages color. I'll remove any explicit fill properties for `IconComponent`. This way, the icon's color will automatically inherit from the button's text color: `midnight` when active, and `slate-text` when inactive. This achieves the desired outline effect without adding unnecessary complexity. It aligns with the user's desire to fit the existing app style perfectly.

payload.text:
SPEC: Additional “Comic Style” Theme Toggle

GOAL
- Let users switch Aura’s UI into a light, playful comic aesthetic without breaking usability.
- Keep it optional, reversible, and compatible with Auri’s personality modes.

SCOPE
- Visual skin only (colors, lines, textures, micro‑motion, Auri styling).
- No layout changes or feature differences.

ENTRY POINTS
- Quick toggle in Profile → Appearance: [Classic] [Comic]
- Command Palette: “Switch to Comic style”
- Optional: onboarding easter egg in Creative Sprint vibe (“Try Comic style”)

DESIGN PRINCIPLES
- Clean comic, not childish. Think “illustrated UI” with soft halftones.
- Maintain contrast/accessibility; WCAG AA minimum.

VISUAL SYSTEM
- Linework: 1.5–2px outlines on key components (cards, buttons, icons).
- Halftone shading: subtle dot/texture in large surfaces (5–8% opacity).
- Color palette:
  - Primary: Mint/Teal as ink accents (#BFF5EA / #00C8C8)
  - Warm highlight: Soft apricot (#FFC7A6) for celebrations
  - Ink: Deep slate (#1E2D2C)
- Shadows: Replace drop shadows with offset “ink” shadows (2–4px, 15%).
- Corners: Slightly rounder radii (+2–4px vs Classic).
- Icons: Switch to outlined Lucide variant with a tiny “bloom” cap.

TYPOGRAPHY
- Keep primary font, but add comic-caption variant for small labels:
  - All-caps micro labels → Sentence case with small bold.
- Speech bubbles: rounded tails, 2px stroke, white fill.

AURI IN COMIC MODE
- Auri gets a thin outline and soft inner texture.
- Micro-emotes: star sparkle, small sweat drop (effort), heart (celebration) — 1–2 frames max.
- Garden mode: add hand-drawn leaves/edges on plants.

MOTION
- Button press: tiny squash/stretch (1.02 scale), spring ease.
- Tooltip/bubble: appear with “page flip” 4–6° rotation.
- Confetti replacement: rising star stickers (5–7 pieces, 700–900ms).

SOUNDS (optional, off by default)
- Soft “paper” rustle on large panel open (‑24 LUFS).
- Subtle “marker tick” on confirm.

ACCESSIBILITY
- High-contrast submode: increase ink line to 2.5px, deepen text contrast.
- Reduced motion: disable page‑flip rotation; keep fades only.

STATE HANDLING
- Persist user choice: ui.theme = "classic" | "comic"
- Respect system dark mode if you have one: comic_dark palette variant with cooler halftones.

SURFACES TO THEME
- Global: background, nav, cards, buttons, inputs, sliders, toggles.
- Auri bubbles, Focus Garden, Studio editor toolbar, Chronos blocks.
- Empty states: swap illustrations to sketchy variants.

COPY (micro)
- Toggle label: “Comic style”
- Tooltip: “A playful illustrated skin. Everything works the same.”

QA CHECKLIST
- Contrast AA on primary text and buttons.
- Focus outlines visible over halftone textures.
- No jitter with spring animations at 60fps.

ROLLBACK
- One-click revert in Appearance.
- Command: “Switch to Classic style”

SUCCESS CRITERIA
- ≥20% of Creative Sprint users try Comic within first week.
- No change in task completion times vs Classic.
- Positive CSAT comments mentioning “fun”/“playful” without “hard to read”.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
- Use CSS variables/tokens for colors, radii, outlines, shadows.
- Add `data-theme="comic"` on root; gate textures and strokes via utility classes.
- Provide themeable SVG icon set (stroke width token).
- Keep all assets lightweight; textures as SVG patterns or tiny PNGs.

**Analyzing Icon Changes**

I'm currently focused on the `MarketView`'s wishlist icon. My goal is to streamline it to a simple heart outline on mobile, maintaining a consistent aesthetic. This requires modifying the icon within the app's framework. I'm exploring the implications of this change on the overall user experience.


**Implementing Mobile Wishlist**

I've made progress on the mobile-specific wishlist icon. I've now introduced conditional rendering within the `MarketView` component, displaying the heart outline exclusively on mobile devices. My recent adjustment involved adding a vertical divider before the wishlist tab, improving visual organization. I also addressed the padding around the heart icon on mobile, enhancing its visual prominence.


**Refining Mobile Flea Market**

I'm tackling the Flea Market Sessions header on mobile. I'm aiming for a more visually pleasing layout where the title and button don't feel cramped. I'm focusing on `TradeView.tsx` where I'll adjust the flexbox properties to improve spacing and alignment, potentially making the "Start Live Session" button more compact. My recent thought is considering the button's vertical size as the source of current awkwardness.


**Optimizing Flea Market Header**

I'm focused on the Flea Market's header on mobile, specifically in `TradeView.tsx`. The current arrangement feels cluttered, especially the "Start Live Session" button. To address this, I'm refactoring the layout to improve balance and readability. I'm exploring ways to integrate the button more seamlessly with the title. Perhaps adjusting flexbox properties will offer a solution, or maybe the button needs to be vertically smaller to avoid dominating the space. My primary thought is to ensure the padding and margins around the header elements are properly balanced.


**Adjusting Flea Market Layout**

I'm making progress on the Flea Market Sessions header layout on mobile. I've been experimenting with `flex-row` and `flex-col` arrangements, along with grid layouts, to optimize the button's placement and the title's visual prominence. The current plan is to use `flex-row` with `justify-between` and `items-center` if space allows, but gracefully stack if needed. I'm focusing on ensuring the button doesn't overwhelm the title, and the overall spacing looks clean and balanced, without the awkwardness I've noticed in previous designs. The goal is a more user-friendly and aesthetically pleasing header.


**Revising Wishlist Icon**

I'm revisiting the wishlist icon's mobile display in `MarketView.tsx`. Initially, I opted for a heart outline. I'm now exploring expanding the heart icon to be a filled-in heart. I'm evaluating its visual impact and potential implications on user comprehension within the existing tab bar structure, making sure it doesn't distract from other functionalities. My key thought is to improve its contrast for greater visibility.


**Enhancing Tab Bar Clarity**

I'm now zeroing in on the tab bar in `MarketView.tsx`, specifically the wishlist icon. I'm focusing on ensuring clear visual distinction. I've been experimenting with `fill` and `stroke` properties for the icon. I'm aiming to keep the desktop version with label, and on mobile, a more visually distinct icon, considering also its integration with the tab bar's visual weight. The mobile-only heart needs to pop without sacrificing the tab bar overall balance.


**Finalizing Mobile Button**

I'm now implementing the adjustments to the Flea Market session header's layout. My focus is on ensuring the "Start Session" button fits seamlessly with the header text on mobile. I'm exploring layout options to achieve a cleaner look and have confirmed I am setting padding and margins to align the elements properly. I've re-examined the button code, ensuring the button remains compact while maintaining its prominence. Also I reviewed the specifics for `MarketView`'s tab changes.


**Optimizing Wishlist Icon**

I'm now implementing the adjustments to the `MarketView`'s wishlist icon. I'm focusing on ensuring better contrast and visual separation from the tab bar on mobile. I've switched from an outline heart to a filled heart. The icon is now using `fill="currentColor"` so it changes with active and inactive tab states. I am confirming adjustments to padding and margins. The goal is to make the icon more immediately recognizable and visually appealing, without disrupting the overall tab bar balance.

**Refining Icon Rendering**

\n\n

I'm now focusing on the dynamic rendering of the `MarketView` icons. The code section now uses conditional logic to display the icons appropriately based on the user's viewport. The desktop version will continue to show a label, but on mobile, I'm making sure to implement the appropriate styles. I'm focusing on ensuring consistent styling. The mobile version is a focus.


**Improving Mobile Button Layout**

\n\n

I'm now focusing on the Flea Market session header's button on mobile, particularly shortening its text. Changing the text to "Start Session" keeps it compact. I've switched the layout to `flex-row` and `justify-between` and ensured a clean presentation and good space utilization. The button now sits more seamlessly next to the title. I'm reviewing the visual styling on the button to ensure the transition is smooth.


**Finalizing Mobile Layout**

I'm now implementing the Flexbox row layout for the Flea Market session header's title and button on mobile. My main aim is to ensure they sit neatly side-by-side. After considering the need for the button's text, I decided to preserve the "Start Session" text to remain descriptive. I've confirmed that the button is now horizontally positioned next to the title, taking up space appropriately. I've re-examined the visual flow and spacing of all elements.

entries.description: Add divider before Wishlist tab, update Wishlist tab to show outline heart icon only on mobile with extra padding
Improve Flea Market Sessions header layout for mobile responsiveness
